function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Orbital AI compute satellites would require inputs from multiple segments. What accounting-policy question would arise once manufacturing begins, and which disclosed policies appear most relevant?

steps.reasoning_content:
The question asks about what accounting-policy question would arise once manufacturing of orbital AI compute satellites begins, and which disclosed policies appear most relevant. This requires me to look at the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand:

1. The segment structure (since orbital AI compute satellites would require inputs from multiple segments)
2. The accounting policies disclosed in the filing
3. What accounting-policy questions would arise from cross-segment manufacturing

Let me first parse the S-1 filing and then retrieve relevant information about segments and accounting policies.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about the segment structure and accounting policies from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What are the reportable segments described in {{spacex_s1}}? List all segments and their descriptions.
What accounting policies are disclosed in {{spacex_s1}} related to inter-segment transactions, intercompany transfers, or cross-segment eliminations?
What accounting policies are disclosed in {{spacex_s1}} related to inventory, manufacturing costs, cost capitalization, or property and equipment?

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 preamble, the company operates **three reportable segments** within its vertically integrated innovation engine. Here are the segments and their descriptions:

1. **Space** 
   * **Description:** This segment designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets to provide high-cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at an unprecedented scale.

2. **Connectivity** 
   * **Description:** This segment operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband data and communications network. It is powered by over 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets.

3. **AI** 
   * **Description:** This segment operates a highly vertically integrated AI platform. It spans the company's truth-seeking frontier model (Grok), AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers, X (its real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform), and AI computational infrastructure.

Based on the provided text, the accounting policies regarding intercompany eliminations and inter-segment transactions are as follows:

*   **Intercompany Eliminations:** All intercompany balances and transactions are eliminated in consolidation. This specifically includes the elimination of all historical related-party transactions between entities that were merged under common control (e.g., SpaceX, xAI, and X).
*   **Inter-segment Revenue Recognition:** The Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue for internal operations. For example, when the Space segment launches the Company's own Starlink satellites, no internal revenue is recorded.
*   **Capitalization of Internal Costs:** Instead of recognizing inter-segment revenue, the costs associated with internal Starlink launches are capitalized as satellites within *Property, plant, and equipment, net* on the consolidated balance sheets.
*   **Segment Revenue Reporting:** The Space segment's reported revenue strictly reflects external customer launches and activities. It excludes the significant amount of launch capacity allocated to internal operations, such as the Connectivity segment and the AI segment.

Based on the provided financial statement notes, here are the accounting policies related to inventory, manufacturing costs, cost capitalization, and property and equipment:

### 1. Inventory
*   **Composition & Classification:** Inventory consists primarily of raw materials, work-in-progress (used in the production of launch vehicles and Starlink Kits), and finished goods (Starlink Kits and Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy second stages awaiting launch). Because the second stages of Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy are not reusable, they are classified as inventory until they are launched or assigned to a revenue transaction.
*   **Valuation:** Inventory is computed using standard cost or weighted average (which approximates actual cost on a first-in, first-out basis) and is stated at the lower of cost or net realizable value.
*   **Write-downs:** The company records inventory write-downs in *Cost of revenue* for estimated obsolescence or unmarketable inventories. These are based on assumptions regarding future demand, design, and technological changes.

### 2. Manufacturing Costs & Cost of Revenue
*   **Cost of Revenue Components:** Includes the cost of materials, depreciation and amortization, shipping and handling, payment processor fees, customs and duties, revenue share costs, infrastructure costs, allocated overhead, and employee compensation (salaries, benefits, share-based compensation).
*   **Infrastructure Costs:** Defined primarily as rocket, kit, and satellite manufacturing facilities, as well as data center costs (lease/hosting, support, maintenance, energy, bandwidth, and public cloud hosting).
*   **Warranties:** The company offers a standard 1-to-2-year warranty on Starlink Kits to repair or replace defective units. An estimate of future warranty costs is recorded in *Cost of revenue* at the time revenue is recognized (though these expenses and liabilities are noted as immaterial).
*   **Other Expenses:** Payment processing fees for credit card sales are included in *Cost of revenue*. Taxes collected and remitted to governments are excluded from the transaction price. Sales commissions with an amortization period of one year or less are expensed as incurred within *Selling, general, and administrative (SG&A)* expenses.

### 3. Cost Capitalization
*   **PP&E Enhancements:** Costs for significant refurbishments, renewals, and enhancements that increase an asset's functionality, output, or expected life are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.
*   **Satellites:** Capitalized costs include the costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as launch costs incurred by the Space segment to get them to orbit (including an allocation of the flight vehicle).
*   **Interest Capitalization:** Interest is capitalized during the construction period for significant long-term projects (e.g., AI infrastructure data centers and launch facilities). Capitalization begins when preparation activities commence, is calculated using average accumulated expenditures and a weighted average borrowing rate, and ceases when portions are substantially completed. Capitalized interest becomes part of the asset's historical cost.
*   **Construction in Progress (CIP):** Includes ongoing construction and expansion of facilities, equipment, and AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service.
*   **Starship Development:** Because Starship is currently in the development stage, a majority of its costs are expensed to *Research and development* as incurred rather than capitalized.

### 4. Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E)
*   **Aerospace Asset Classification:**
    *   **Reusable (PP&E):** Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy boosters (first stages), fairings, Merlin engines, and fully reusable Dragon capsules.
    *   **Expendable (Inventory):** Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy second stages.
*   **Valuation & Depreciation Methods:** PP&E is stated at cost less accumulated depreciation. 
    *   *Standard Assets:* Depreciated using the straight-line method over estimated useful lives.
    *   *Flight Vehicles & Spacecraft:* Depreciated based on the **expected number of average flights**. Management estimates this by considering recovery/refurbishment success rates, economics, customer acceptance limits, mission manifests, and retirement timing (e.g., transitioning from Falcon to Starship).
    *   *Satellites:* Depreciation factors in on-orbit performance, orbit-raise timing, service capability, and constellation evolution.
    *   *Leasehold Improvements:* Depreciated over the shorter of their estimated useful lives or the related lease term.
    *   *Adjustments:* If useful lives or expected remaining flights change, the depreciation rate is adjusted prospectively.
*   **Estimated Useful Lives Schedule:**
    *   Servers and networking equipment: 5 - 6 years
    *   Satellites: 3 - 5 years
    *   Machinery and equipment: 3 - 10 years
    *   Flight vehicle hardware: 5 - 25 flights
    *   Data center infrastructure: 20 - 25 years
    *   Launch sites: 7 - 20 years
    *   Buildings and improvements: 30 years
    *   Leasehold improvements: Shorter of 7 - 20 years or the life of the lease
*   **Impairment & Disposals:**
    *   Evaluated at the lowest level of independent cash flows whenever events indicate the carrying value may not be recoverable (e.g., technological changes, negative trends, or changes in asset use).
    *   If carrying value exceeds estimated fair value, an impairment charge is recognized.
    *   *Routine* disposals, scrapping, gateway decommissions, and recurring operational losses are charged to *Cost of revenue* or *SG&A*.
    *   *Abnormal* impairments (outside the normal course of business) are charged to *Impairment* (e.g., a $24 million write-off of damaged flight vehicles in the Space segment in Q1 2025).

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a good understanding of the segments and accounting policies. Let me retrieve more specific information about how the company handles cross-segment costs, particularly for satellites and any mention of orbital AI compute satellites.

arguments.prompt: What does {{spacex_s1}} say about orbital AI compute satellites, space-based AI infrastructure, or AI compute in orbit? Any mention of satellites used for AI computation?
What does {{spacex_s1}} say about how satellite costs are allocated between segments, or how costs are attributed when one segment provides goods or services to another segment?

results.content:
**Yes, absolutely.** The provided text extensively, explicitly, and repeatedly details the company's strategic vision for **orbital AI compute satellites**, **space-based AI infrastructure**, and **AI compute in orbit**. 

In fact, moving AI computation into space is presented as a core multi-trillion-dollar business pillar and the ultimate solution to Earth's terrestrial energy and infrastructure constraints. 

Here is a breakdown of exactly what the text says regarding these concepts:

### 1. Orbital AI Compute Satellites
The text provides specific engineering and operational details about the satellites designed explicitly for AI computation:
* **Hardware & Design:** The company is evolving its "V3 satellite platform" to host "electronics centered on AI accelerators rather than communications processors." These satellites will require "substantially larger radiators for thermal management" and "significantly larger solar arrays" (generating roughly 100 kW of compute power per metric ton).
* **Deployment Timeline:** The company explicitly states, *"We expect to begin deploying our orbital AI compute satellites as early as 2028."*
* **Networking:** The satellites will be interconnected using Starlink's existing "inter-satellite lasers that create a dynamic mesh network in space," allowing AI data traffic to route through orbit rather than relying on terrestrial backhaul.

### 2. Space-Based AI Infrastructure & Orbital Data Centers
The text frames space-based infrastructure as the only viable path to achieve Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) and meet the immense power demands of future AI models:
* **Orbital Data Centers:** The company plans to build "orbital data centers" and "modular orbital AI compute shells" in Sun-synchronous orbit. 
* **Unlimited Power & Free Cooling:** The core thesis is that Earth's finite resources cannot sustain AI's energy demands. Space offers the "ultimate fusion energy source: the Sun" (near-constant, uninterrupted solar energy) and allows for "radiative cooling architectures, which incur no operating costs compared to liquid or air cooling."
* **Massive Scale (100 Gigawatts):** The long-term ambition is to deploy **100 gigawatts** of AI compute capacity to orbit annually (noted as roughly one-fifth of total U.S. power production in 2025). 

### 3. Satellites Used for AI Computation
The text addresses the unique challenges and solutions of using satellites as flying data centers:
* **High-Density Payloads:** The company is designing "next-generation, high-performance AI compute satellites" and "new satellites that can host processors for high-density compute payloads."
* **Fault Tolerance & Fleet Management:** Because orbital infrastructure "will not be readily accessible" and cannot be easily repaired, the company will conduct "intensive pre-deployment testing" on Earth to weed out early silicon defects. If a chip fails in orbit, they will use "existing Starlink fleet management software to reallocate traffic to other satellites and prevent cluster-level downtime."
* **Custom Silicon:** The company mentions designing "chips that are optimized for the space environment" and leveraging proprietary chips to operate AI-focused electronics in space.

### 4. The Enabler: Starship and Vertical Integration
The text makes it clear that this space-based AI infrastructure is entirely dependent on the company's aerospace capabilities (clearly identifying the entity as SpaceX, following its acquisition of xAI):
* **Launch Cadence:** Deploying 100 GW of compute requires transporting "approximately one million metric tons to orbit annually," which necessitates "thousands of launches per year."
* **Starship's Role:** This is only possible via the "fully reusable nature of Starship," with V3 carrying 100 metric tons and future V4 generations carrying up to 200 metric tons.
* **The Ultimate Moat:** The text argues that *"physical infrastructure, not models, will be the primary competitive differentiator for AI companies,"* and claims they are the *"only company with a commercially viable path to building orbital AI compute at scale"* due to their extreme vertical integration across rockets, satellite manufacturing, and the Starlink network. 

**Summary:** 
The text outlines a highly detailed, heavily capitalized roadmap to launch millions of solar-powered, AI-accelerator-equipped satellites into orbit. The stated goal is to bypass terrestrial power grid limitations, drastically reduce the cost of AI "tokens," and secure a permanent, unreplicable monopoly on the physical infrastructure required to train and run frontier AI models like Grok.

Based on the provided text, the company handles inter-segment costs and allocations—specifically when the Space segment provides launch services for the Connectivity and AI segments—using the following accounting treatments:

*   **No Inter-Segment Revenue Recognition:** The Space segment does not recognize any inter-segment revenue when it launches internal payloads, such as Starlink satellites for the Connectivity segment or future payloads for the AI segment. Its reported revenue strictly reflects external customer launches and activities.
*   **Capitalization of Launch Costs:** Instead of recognizing revenue, the launch costs incurred by the Space segment to deploy internal satellites are capitalized directly within the receiving segment (e.g., the Connectivity segment). These costs, which include an allocation of the flight vehicle costs, are added to the satellite's asset value in "Property, plant, and equipment, net."
*   **Depreciation in the Receiving Segment:** Once capitalized, these launch costs are depreciated over time within the receiving segment's financial results, rather than being recorded as an immediate expense or an inter-segment charge.
*   **Future Starship Cost Allocation:** The text notes that once the next-generation Starship vehicle is commercialized, its costs will generally be capitalized and then depreciated in the cost of revenue of the specific segment associated with the payload being delivered. 

In summary, internal launches are treated as capital expenditures for the receiving segment rather than revenue-generating transactions for the Space segment, meaning the Space segment's financial results do not reflect the "foundational strategic value" it provides to the rest of the company.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a very clear picture. Let me also look for more specific accounting policy disclosures about how costs are allocated when manufacturing involves inputs from multiple segments, and whether there's any specific discussion about the accounting treatment for orbital AI compute satellites.

arguments.prompt: What does {{spacex_s1}} say about the accounting policy for determining which segment an asset belongs to, or how assets are classified across segments? Any discussion of segment asset attribution?
What does {{spacex_s1}} say about how the company determines segment profitability, segment adjusted EBITDA, or segment operating results? What costs are included in each segment?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, there is **no discussion or accounting policy detailed regarding segment asset attribution**, how balance sheet assets are classified across the Space, Connectivity, and AI segments, or how the company determines which segment an asset belongs to. 

The text explicitly notes that the Chief Operating Decision Maker (CODM) evaluates performance and allocates resources based on **segment income (loss) from operations** (and Segment Adjusted EBITDA). It also mentions that the CODM *"does not evaluate operating and reportable segments using..."* (the sentence cuts off, but in standard financial reporting, this typically concludes with "total assets"). 

While the segment reporting tables include metrics like *Depreciation and amortization* and *Capital expenditures*, the provided excerpts do not contain the underlying accounting policies for attributing or allocating consolidated assets to specific reportable segments.

Based on the provided text, here is how the company determines segment profitability and Segment Adjusted EBITDA, along with the specific costs associated with each segment:

### 1. Determination of Segment Profitability and Segment Adjusted EBITDA
The company evaluates segment operating results using a specific GAAP starting point and then applies non-GAAP adjustments to reflect ongoing operational performance:
*   **Starting Point (GAAP):** The foundation for segment profitability is **Segment Income (Loss) from Operations**, which the company identifies as the most directly comparable GAAP measure.
*   **Calculating Segment Adjusted EBITDA (Non-GAAP):** To arrive at Segment Adjusted EBITDA, management adds back/excludes four specific items from the Segment Income (Loss) from Operations:
    1.  Depreciation and amortization
    2.  Share-based compensation
    3.  Restructuring charges
    4.  Impairments
*   **Purpose:** Management uses Segment Adjusted EBITDA for internal planning, forecasting, and assessing core financial performance because it removes the impact of items that do not directly reflect ongoing operating results. 

### 2. Costs Included in Each Segment
The Management’s Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) highlights the primary costs and investment drivers that impact the operating results of each specific segment:

**Space Segment**
*   **Research & Development (R&D):** Massive R&D expenses dedicated to the next-generation Starship launch vehicle program (e.g., $3.0 billion in 2025 and $930 million in Q1 2026).
*   **Infrastructure:** Investments in launch site infrastructure and facilities to support future Starship launches.
*   **Operating Costs:** Standard cost of revenue, selling, general, and administrative expenses. 
*   *Note:* The segment's cost efficiency is heavily driven by the reusability and reliability of its rockets.

**Connectivity Segment (Starlink)**
*   **Depreciation:** Depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs, which scales with the increase in Starlink flights.
*   **Growth & Expansion:** Marketing and international expansion costs aimed at driving subscriber growth.
*   **Hardware Production:** User terminal production costs (though the company notes its highly verticalized supply chain has driven major cost reductions in this area).

**AI Segment (xAI)**
*   **Compute & Hardware:** Cloud computing costs, GPU depreciation costs, and massive capital expenditures for terrestrial and planned orbital data centers.
*   **Facilities & Energy:** Data center infrastructure, facilities-related costs, and energy procurement.
*   **Personnel:** Employee compensation expenses and costs for technical personnel.
*   *Note:* The AI segment is in an earlier stage of development, and the company expects a "multi-year investment horizon" of heavy capital deployment before the segment achieves sustained positive Adjusted EBITDA.

### 3. Context on Segment Operating Results
*   **Vertical Integration:** The text notes that the company operates a "vertically integrated business model." While the exact inter-segment billing mechanics (e.g., how the Space segment charges the Connectivity segment for launches) are not detailed in this excerpt, the Space and Connectivity segments benefit from immense scale and operating leverage, contributing the substantial majority of consolidated revenue.
*   **Investment Cycles:** Segment profitability is highly sensitive to the company's capital allocation and development cycles. For example, the Space segment's Adjusted EBITDA fluctuates based on the intensity of its Starship R&D phases, while the AI segment is currently operating at a significant loss due to the rapid, cost-effective scaling of compute infrastructure required to capture a massive market opportunity.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also check if there's any specific discussion about the accounting treatment for the orbital AI compute satellites - specifically which segment they would be attributed to.

arguments.prompt: What does {{spacex_s1}} say about which segment orbital AI compute satellites would be classified under, or how the company would account for satellites that serve both connectivity and AI compute functions?
What does {{spacex_s1}} say about the CODM, chief operating decision maker, and how segment assets or capital expenditures are evaluated? Does the CODM review assets by segment?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is how the company addresses the classification and accounting of orbital AI compute satellites:

**1. Segment Classification for Orbital AI Compute Satellites**
While the text does not explicitly state "orbital AI compute satellites are classified under the AI segment," it strongly implies this classification through the segment definitions and operational plans:
*   **The AI Segment** is defined as operating a vertically integrated platform that includes **"AI computational infrastructure."** 
*   **The Space Segment** (which handles manufacturing and launches) notes that it currently allocates launch capacity to the Connectivity segment and expects to **"allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future,"** aligning with the planned deployment of orbital AI data centers. 
*   Therefore, the orbital AI compute infrastructure would be owned/operated by the **AI segment**, while the **Space segment** would act as the internal launch provider.

**2. Accounting for "Dual-Purpose" Satellites**
The provided text **does not contain an accounting policy** for satellites that serve both connectivity and AI compute functions simultaneously. In fact, the text suggests that the company views these as distinct, specialized assets rather than hybrid ones. It explicitly notes that there are **"material differences between connectivity satellites and AI compute satellites,"** highlighting that AI compute satellites are optimized for high-performance computing and involve **"the removal of much of the communications hardware"** found on standard connectivity satellites. 

**3. Inter-Segment Accounting for Satellite Launches**
The text does explain how the company accounts for the internal deployment of satellites (which would apply to both Connectivity and future AI compute satellites):
*   **No Inter-Segment Revenue:** When the Space segment launches satellites for internal use (such as Starlink or future AI constellations), it "does not recognize any inter-segment revenue."
*   **Capitalization:** Instead, those launch costs are "capitalized in satellites in *Property, plant, and equipment, net*" on the balance sheet of the receiving segment (Connectivity or AI). 
*   **Space Segment Revenue:** The Space segment's revenue "only reflects our customer launches and customer activities," keeping internal infrastructure deployments strictly as capitalized costs rather than recognized revenue.

Based on the provided text, here is what is detailed regarding the Chief Operating Decision Maker (CODM), segment assets, and capital expenditures:

### **Role of the CODM**
* **Identity:** The Company’s Chief Executive Officer serves as the CODM.
* **Responsibilities:** The CODM organizes the company, manages resource allocations, and measures performance across three operating and reportable segments: **(i) Space, (ii) Connectivity, and (iii) AI**. 

### **Performance Evaluation & Resource Allocation**
* **Primary Metric:** The CODM assesses performance and allocates resources based on **segment income (loss) from operations**. 
* **Methodology:** This is done by comparing actual income (loss) from operations against historical results and previously forecasted financial information.

### **Does the CODM review assets by segment?**
* **The text is truncated:** The provided excerpt cuts off mid-sentence regarding the evaluation of segment assets. It states: *"The Company’s CODM does not evaluate operating and reportable segments using..."* and then abruptly ends. 
* Because of this cut-off, the text does not explicitly confirm the final metric (though in standard SEC filings, this sentence typically concludes with "total assets"). Therefore, based *strictly* on the provided text, it is explicitly stated that the CODM does **not** use a specific (but unnamed/truncated) metric to evaluate the segments, and segment assets are not listed in the segment reporting tables provided.

### **How Capital Expenditures are Evaluated and Reported**
While the specific sentence defining the CODM's metrics cuts off before mentioning capital expenditures, the text heavily emphasizes that the CODM **"manages resource allocations,"** and the document extensively tracks capital expenditures at the segment level to reflect this:
* **Segment-Level Tracking:** The company provides detailed tables breaking down capital expenditures specifically by the Space, Connectivity, and AI segments.
* **Strategic Resource Allocation (MD&A Analysis):** The text provides deep-dive variance analyses into *why* capital is being allocated to each segment, showing massive year-over-year growth in segment-specific infrastructure:
  * **Space:** Driven by increased investments in launch site infrastructure for Starship.
  * **Connectivity:** Driven by higher satellite and ground equipment costs to grow the satellite network.
  * **AI:** Driven by the rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and compute infrastructure. 

In summary, the CODM relies on **segment income (loss) from operations** to measure performance, while **capital expenditures** are rigorously tracked and reported by segment to illustrate how resources are being allocated to fund the company's massive infrastructure build-outs. The text explicitly begins to state that the CODM does *not* evaluate segments using a certain metric (likely total assets), but the sentence is cut off in the provided excerpts.

arguments.prompt: What does {{spacex_s1}} say about who manufactures the satellites? Does the Space segment or Connectivity segment manufacture Starlink satellites? Where is satellite manufacturing done?
What does {{spacex_s1}} say about the V3 satellite platform, satellite manufacturing capabilities, or who builds the satellites?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the details regarding the manufacturing of Starlink satellites:

**Who manufactures the satellites?**
SpaceX manufactures the satellites entirely in-house. The text emphasizes the company's "extreme vertical integration," noting that unlike traditional satellite manufacturers that rely on fragmented supply chains and outsourced production, SpaceX has "built an integrated satellite platform that spans architecture, chip design, software, power systems, and final assembly." 

**Does the Space segment or Connectivity segment manufacture them?**
The text does not explicitly assign the *manufacturing* of the satellites to the financial operations of a single segment, but it draws a clear line between rockets and satellites:
*   **The Space Segment** is explicitly defined as the segment that "designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets."
*   **The Connectivity Segment** "operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites." 
*   While satellite manufacturing is described as a core, company-wide capability ("Unrivaled Satellite and Connectivity Platform across Design, Manufacturing...") that enables the Connectivity and AI segments, the financial burden of the satellites falls on the **Connectivity segment**. The text notes that for launches dedicated to deploying Starlink satellites, the company capitalizes the associated costs within the Connectivity segment and depreciates them over time. 

**Where is the manufacturing done?**
The text identifies specific facilities dedicated to Starlink manufacturing:
*   **Redmond, Washington:** This is the primary **Starlink satellite manufacturing facility**. It produces the actual satellites (averaging approximately 70 satellites per week, or 3,640 per year at full rate), including "bus structures, phased-array antennas, propulsion, solar arrays, and inter-satellite lasers."
*   **Bastrop, Texas:** This facility manufactures the "majority of Starlink products," specifically producing tens of thousands of **Starlink Kits** (user terminals) per day. The text also notes that in 2026, this facility will expand to produce Starlink gateway antennas, solar cells, and AI compute satellites. 
*   *(Note: The text also mentions that SpaceX produces Starship heatshield tiles at "the Bakery" in southern California and maintains corporate/manufacturing operations in Hawthorne, California, but Redmond and Bastrop are the specific hubs named for Starlink constellation and product manufacturing).*

Based on the provided text, here is the information regarding the V3 satellite platform, SpaceX's manufacturing capabilities, and who builds the satellites:

### **The V3 Satellite Platform**
*   **Core Capabilities:** The core V3 design is complete and incorporates proprietary chips. For broadband, a single V3 satellite is designed to offer **1 Tbps of downlink capacity**. 
*   **Orbital AI Compute Variant:** The V3 platform serves as the foundation for SpaceX's orbital AI compute satellites. The AI variants will replace communications hardware with AI accelerators, larger radiators for thermal management, and significantly larger solar arrays (requiring approximately 5x the solar output of standard V3 designs) to generate roughly **100 kW of compute power per metric ton**.
*   **Deployment & Scale:** Deployment is expected to begin in the **second half of 2026** using the Starship rocket. A single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to **60 V3 satellites** to Low-Earth Orbit (LEO), representing a 20-fold increase in deployed capacity compared to a Falcon 9 launch.
*   **Cost Reductions:** Through vertical integration, SpaceX expects to achieve a **9x reduction** in manufacturing costs per Gbps of downlink capacity when moving from V1 Broadband satellites to V3 satellites.

### **Satellite Manufacturing Capabilities**
*   **Automotive-Style Scale:** SpaceX has built one of the largest satellite manufacturing operations in the world. They utilize standardized bus architectures, rapid iteration cycles, and **"automotive-style production lines"** to mass-produce thousands of satellites per year.
*   **Extreme Vertical Integration:** Unlike traditional aerospace companies, SpaceX's manufacturing spans architecture, chip design, software, power systems, and final assembly. This high degree of vertical integration allows them to rapidly evolve subsystem designs, quickly deploy the latest AI processors, and operate with limited reliance on third-party suppliers.
*   **Facilities:** Manufacturing is performed in-house at SpaceX facilities, including their original flagship facility in **Hawthorne, California**, which handles high-reliability production for various spacecraft, engines, and components. 

### **Who Builds the Satellites?**
*   **SpaceX (In-House):** **SpaceX builds the satellites themselves.** The text explicitly contrasts SpaceX's approach with traditional satellite manufacturers and competitors who rely on fragmented supply chains, low-volume production, and outsourced manufacturers to build architectures comparable to satellites SpaceX "retired years ago." 
*   **Chip Fabrication Partners (Terafab):** While SpaceX builds and integrates the actual satellites in-house, they are partnering to secure the massive amount of silicon required to power the AI payloads. SpaceX announced the **Terafab initiative** in collaboration with **Tesla** and **Intel** (which joined in April 2026). Intel contributes expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high-performance chips optimized for the space environment, with a long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute hardware annually to prevent future chip shortages.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a very comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize the answer.

The key accounting-policy question that would arise once manufacturing of orbital AI compute satellites begins is:

**Which segment should bear the manufacturing costs and own the resulting assets?**

Here's why this is a significant accounting-policy question:

1. **Multi-segment inputs**: Orbital AI compute satellites would require inputs from multiple segments:
   - **Space segment**: Designs and manufactures rockets, provides launch services. The satellite bus/platform manufacturing appears to be done at facilities that are part of the broader SpaceX manufacturing operation (Redmond, Hawthorne, Bastrop).
   - **Connectivity segment**: Operates the Starlink satellite network, has the satellite manufacturing expertise and facilities (Redmond), and the V3 platform is the base for both connectivity and AI compute variants.
   - **AI segment**: Would ultimately operate the AI compute payloads and use the computational output.

2. **Current disclosed policy for inter-segment transactions**:
   - No inter-segment revenue is recognized
   - Launch costs are capitalized in the receiving segment's PP&E
   - The Space segment's revenue only reflects external customer launches

3. **The accounting-policy question**: When manufacturing begins, the company would need to determine:
   - **Asset classification/segment attribution**: Which segment "owns" the orbital AI compute satellites? The AI segment (because they provide AI compute infrastructure) or the Connectivity segment (because they share the V3 platform, satellite bus, inter-satellite laser mesh network, and fleet management software)?
   - **Cost allocation during manufacturing**: The satellites would be manufactured using shared facilities, shared V3 platform architecture, shared chip design (Terafab), and potentially shared production lines. How should manufacturing costs be allocated between segments during the construction phase?
   - **Capitalization policy**: Currently, satellite costs (parts, labor, allocated overhead) and launch costs are capitalized. But for orbital AI compute satellites, which segment's capital expenditures and PP&E would they appear in?
   - **Depreciation**: The useful life and depreciation method would need to be determined - would they follow the satellite depreciation policy (3-5 years) or the data center infrastructure policy (20-25 years)?

The most relevant disclosed policies are:
1. **Inter-segment transaction elimination policy** (no inter-segment revenue; costs capitalized in receiving segment)
2. **Satellite cost capitalization policy** (parts, labor, allocated overhead, and launch costs capitalized as PP&E)
3. **PP&E classification and depreciation policies** (satellites: 3-5 years; servers/networking: 5-6 years; data center infrastructure: 20-25 years)
4. **Segment reporting policy** (CODM evaluates based on segment income from operations, not total assets)
5. **Inventory policy** (raw materials, WIP, finished goods at lower of cost or NRV)

The filing does NOT explicitly address how the company would account for assets that span multiple segments' functional domains - i.e., a satellite that is manufactured using Connectivity segment expertise and facilities, launched by the Space segment, but operated for AI compute purposes by the AI segment.

Let me also check if there's anything about the Bastrop facility expanding to produce AI compute satellites and which segment that would fall under.

What does {{spacex_s1}} say about shared facilities, shared costs, or overhead allocation between segments? How are corporate or shared costs allocated?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is what is said about the Bastrop facility and its segment attribution:

### **Bastrop's Production of AI Compute Satellites**
The text states that in 2026, the company expects to **more than double the size of the Bastrop facility**. This expansion is designed to support new Starlink products and deepen vertical integration by adding the production of:
*   **AI compute satellites**
*   Starlink gateway antennas
*   Solar cells 

### **Segment Attribution**
While the text does not explicitly assign Bastrop to a single segment in one sentence, it provides clear context that attributes the facility primarily to the **Connectivity** and **Space** segments, while acting as a manufacturing supplier for the **AI** segment:

*   **The Connectivity Segment:** The text explicitly introduces Bastrop as the facility where the company builds *"the majority of Starlink products,"* including *"tens of thousands of Starlink Kits [user terminals] per day."* Later in the financial overview, the company directly links user terminal production and supply chain efficiencies to the Connectivity segment (e.g., *"Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA... highly verticalized supply chain, which has delivered major cost reductions in user terminal production"*). 
*   **The Space Segment:** Because Bastrop is expanding to manufacture the actual spacecraft hardware (AI compute satellites) and space-grade solar cells, it heavily supports the **Space segment**, which encompasses the company's satellite manufacturing and orbital deployment operations.
*   **The AI Segment (as a supplier):** Although Bastrop will produce *AI compute satellites*, the text indicates that the **AI Segment's** financial results and infrastructure investments are currently driven by terrestrial assets (e.g., *"higher cloud compute and data center infrastructure,"* and the COLOSSUS terrestrial data centers). Therefore, Bastrop would not be classified as an AI Segment facility; rather, it is a Space/Connectivity manufacturing hub that supplies the orbital hardware necessary for the company's broader AI compute strategy. 

**Summary:** Bastrop is fundamentally a **Connectivity/Space segment** manufacturing hub (as the core Starlink production facility) that is expanding its vertical integration to supply the orbital hardware required for the company's **AI segment** ambitions.

Based on the provided financial and operational disclosures, the company allocates shared facilities, corporate overhead, and shared production costs across its Space, Connectivity, and AI segments. While the exact mathematical methodology for these allocations is not detailed, the text highlights several key areas where shared costs and overhead are applied:

### 1. Selling, General, and Administrative (SG&A) Overhead
*   **Corporate Functions:** SG&A expenses across the segments include **allocated employee compensation costs** for shared administrative functions, specifically sales, facilities, legal, finance, information technology, and human resources. 
*   **Corporate Assets:** The Space segment’s SG&A explicitly includes **corporate aircraft costs**.
*   **Fluctuations in Allocated Overhead:** The MD&A notes year-over-year changes in allocated general and administrative overhead. For example, in 2025, there was a $52 million decrease in allocated G&A overhead, while in 2024, the Connectivity segment saw a $54 million increase in allocated G&A overhead.

### 2. Cost of Revenue & Infrastructure
*   **Infrastructure & Facilities:** Cost of revenue includes **allocated overhead** and **infrastructure costs**. These infrastructure costs consist of shared rocket, kit, and satellite manufacturing facilities, as well as data center/colocated facility costs (including lease, hosting, energy, and bandwidth costs).
*   **Launch Operations Capitalization:** Launch operations and overhead costs are sometimes capitalized into the Connectivity segment when they relate to internal Starlink satellite launches. In 2025, an increase to 122 Starlink launches resulted in **$14 million** of launch operations and overhead costs being capitalized to the Connectivity segment.
*   **Hardware Production:** The Space segment's cost of revenue includes **shared costs incurred in the production of launch hardware**.

### 3. Research & Development (R&D) and Revenue Recognition
*   **R&D Overhead:** The Connectivity segment’s R&D expenses include **allocated overhead** alongside employee/contractor compensation and equipment leases.
*   **Contract Estimations (Cost-to-Cost Method):** For contracts recognized over time, management must use significant judgment to estimate total costs at completion. This explicitly requires assumptions regarding the **allocation of shared costs**, such as:
    *   *Space:* Allocation of shared costs for launch vehicles identified as reusable for multiple launches.
    *   *Connectivity:* Allocation of shared costs used in the production of satellites.

### 4. Cross-Segment Synergies and Shared Initiatives
*   **Vertical Integration & Shared Culture:** The AI segment (xAI) notes that its ability to build gigawatt-scale compute infrastructure (COLOSSUS) at record speeds and lower costs stems from "complete vertical integration and the **shared culture** infused by our founder, Mr. Musk, across our Space, Connectivity, and AI segments."
*   **Terafab Initiative:** The text mentions **Terafab**, a joint initiative together with Tesla to build a manufacturing facility capable of producing 1 terawatt per year of compute hardware, indicating shared or partnered infrastructure development across the broader Musk ecosystem. 

*Note: The provided text explains the **impact and categories** of these allocated costs (e.g., capitalizing launch overhead to Connectivity, or allocating corporate HR/IT to SG&A), but it does not disclose the specific internal transfer pricing or accounting formulas used to calculate the exact allocation rates between the segments.*

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a very comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize the answer.

The key accounting-policy question that would arise once manufacturing of orbital AI compute satellites begins is about **segment asset attribution and cost allocation** for an asset that inherently spans multiple segments.

Here's the core issue:

1. **Manufacturing inputs come from multiple segments:**
   - The **Space segment** provides launch services and potentially some manufacturing capabilities
   - The **Connectivity segment** provides the V3 satellite platform, satellite manufacturing facilities (Redmond, Bastrop), inter-satellite laser mesh network, and fleet management software
   - The **AI segment** provides the AI accelerator payloads, custom chip design, and would operate the compute infrastructure

2. **The accounting-policy question:** Once manufacturing begins, the company must determine:
   - **Which segment should capitalize and own the orbital AI compute satellite assets?** The satellites are manufactured using Connectivity segment facilities and expertise, launched by the Space segment, but operated for AI compute purposes by the AI segment.
   - **How should manufacturing costs be allocated during construction?** The V3 platform is shared between connectivity and AI compute variants, and Bastrop (a Connectivity/Space facility) will produce AI compute satellites.
   - **What depreciation policy applies?** Satellites are depreciated over 3-5 years, but data center infrastructure is depreciated over 20-25 years. Orbital AI compute satellites are essentially "flying data centers" - which useful life applies?

3. **The most relevant disclosed policies:**
   - **Inter-segment transaction policy**: No inter-segment revenue; costs capitalized in receiving segment's PP&E
   - **Satellite cost capitalization policy**: Parts, labor, allocated overhead, and launch costs capitalized
   - **PP&E classification and depreciation**: Satellites (3-5 years) vs. data center infrastructure (20-25 years) vs. servers/networking (5-6 years)
   - **Shared cost allocation**: Overhead allocated across segments, but specific methodology not disclosed
   - **Segment reporting**: CODM evaluates based on segment income from operations, not total assets

The filing does NOT explicitly resolve these questions. The filing describes the orbital AI compute satellites as a future initiative (deployment expected as early as 2028), and the current accounting policies were designed for a world where satellites serve a single purpose (connectivity) and are clearly attributable to one segment.

arguments.final_result:
## Accounting-Policy Question for Orbital AI Compute Satellites

### The Core Question: Segment Asset Attribution and Cost Allocation

Once manufacturing of orbital AI compute satellites begins, the central accounting-policy question is: **Which reportable segment should capitalize, own, and depreciate these assets, given that their manufacturing, deployment, and operation draw on resources and capabilities from all three segments?**

This question arises because orbital AI compute satellites are inherently cross-segment assets:

| Input Source | Segment | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing facilities** | Connectivity / Space | Bastrop, TX facility (a Connectivity/Space manufacturing hub) is expanding in 2026 to produce AI compute satellites; Redmond, WA produces satellite buses |
| **Satellite platform** | Connectivity | V3 platform architecture, inter-satellite laser mesh network, fleet-management software |
| **Launch services** | Space | Starship deployment (up to 60 V3 satellites per launch) |
| **AI compute payload** | AI | AI accelerators replacing communications hardware, custom silicon (Terafab initiative), Grok model operations |
| **Thermal / power systems** | Shared | Substantially larger radiators and solar arrays (~100 kW per metric ton) unique to AI compute variant |

The filing does **not** disclose an accounting policy that resolves how an asset spanning all three segments' functional domains should be attributed for segment reporting purposes.

---

### Most Relevant Disclosed Policies

**1. Inter-Segment Transaction Elimination Policy**
- The company does **not** recognize inter-segment revenue. When the Space segment launches internal payloads, no revenue is recorded; instead, launch costs (including an allocation of the flight vehicle) are **capitalized as satellites in PP&E** on the consolidated balance sheet of the receiving segment.
- *Relevance:* This policy governs how the Space segment's launch contribution to orbital AI compute deployment would be recorded—but it does not address which segment is the "receiving segment" when the asset serves AI compute rather than connectivity.

**2. Satellite Cost Capitalization Policy**
- Capitalized satellite costs include **parts, labor, allocated overhead, and launch costs** (including an allocation of the flight vehicle). These are recorded within *Property, plant, and equipment, net*.
- *Relevance:* Manufacturing at Bastrop and Redmond would generate costs that must be accumulated and capitalized—but the policy does not specify which segment's capital expenditure line these costs flow through during construction.

**3. PP&E Classification and Depreciation Policies**
- The filing discloses materially different useful lives for asset categories that orbital AI compute satellites straddle:
  - **Satellites:** 3–5 years
  - **Servers and networking equipment:** 5–6 years
  - **Data center infrastructure:** 20–25 years
- Flight vehicles and spacecraft are depreciated based on **expected number of average flights** rather than straight-line time.
- *Relevance:* Orbital AI compute satellites are described as "orbital data centers" and "modular orbital AI compute shells"—functionally flying data centers. The filing does not specify whether these would be depreciated as satellites (3–5 years), as data center infrastructure (20–25 years), or under some hybrid methodology. This choice would have a material impact on segment-level depreciation expense and Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

**4. Shared Cost and Overhead Allocation**
- Cost of revenue includes **allocated overhead** and **infrastructure costs** (shared manufacturing facilities, data center costs). The MD&A references year-over-year changes in allocated G&A overhead across segments (e.g., a $52 million decrease in 2025, a $54 million increase to Connectivity in 2024), but the **specific allocation methodology is not disclosed**.
- *Relevance:* Shared manufacturing lines at Bastrop producing both Starlink Kits and AI compute satellites would require a cost-allocation methodology that the filing does not currently describe.

**5. Segment Reporting Framework**
- The CODM (CEO) evaluates segments based on **segment income (loss) from operations** and Segment Adjusted EBITDA (which adds back depreciation, share-based compensation, restructuring, and impairments). The filing states the CODM **does not evaluate segments using [total assets]** (sentence truncated but consistent with standard ASC 280 disclosure).
- *Relevance:* Because the CODM does not review segment-level assets, the attribution of orbital AI compute satellite PP&E to a specific segment may not be visible in segment reporting—yet the depreciation flowing through that segment's income from operations would directly affect Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

---

### Why This Is Materially Unresolved

The filing explicitly notes **"material differences between connectivity satellites and AI compute satellites,"** including the removal of communications hardware and the addition of AI accelerators, larger radiators, and significantly larger solar arrays. This suggests these are **distinct asset classes**, not minor variants of existing Starlink satellites. Yet the current accounting policies were designed for a paradigm in which:

- Satellites serve a single purpose (connectivity)
- The receiving segment is unambiguous (Connectivity)
- Depreciation follows the satellite useful-life schedule (3–5 years)

Once manufacturing begins (Bastrop expansion in 2026; deployment expected as early as 2028), the company will need to establish and disclose:

1. **Segment attribution policy** for assets manufactured by one segment, launched by another, and operated by a third
2. **Depreciation methodology** for orbital compute infrastructure that functionally serves as a data center but physically operates as a satellite
3. **Cost-allocation methodology** for shared manufacturing facilities producing both connectivity and AI compute hardware

The filing does not provide sufficient information to conclude how these questions will be resolved.

---

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}
